summary: Sugaree monster, Lost/Saint experiment, solid second set
blurb: The show delivers a monster Sugaree and an early Lost Sailor > Saint Of Circumstance pairing that showcases the band's exploratory vibe. The second set shines with China Cat Sunflower > I Know You Rider and a tight Drum/Space → Wharf Rat sequence, ending with a lively Shakedown Street encore.
review: On September 4 1979 at Madison Square Garden the Dead put on a performance that swings between brilliance and unevenness. Jerry opened strong with “Jack Straw” – his noodling described as supreme – and followed with a soaring, perfectly‑tuned “Sugaree” that many listeners call a monster of the era. The early‑set pairing of “Lost Sailor” into “Saint Of Circumstance” shows the band experimenting with seamless segues, while “Deal” and “China Cat Sunflower > I Know You Rider” keep the energy high. The second set’s highlight is the classic “He’s Gone” launch into a powerful Drum/Space segment that leads into an inspired “Wharf Rat”, capped off by a rousing “Shakedown Street” encore. Recording quality varies – the Miller soundboard capture (gd1979‑09‑04.sbd.miller.79747.flac16) offers crystal‑clear balance despite occasional crowd noise, making it the recommended listen.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Three recordings label the show as mixed, praising specific songs and segues while noting crowd noise and occasional performance dips; one recording is negative. The overall sentiment balances out with no clear consensus for strong positive or negative direction, resulting in a neutral adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Soundboard source with crystal‑clear balance; captures the band’s dynamics despite occasional crowd noise, and is widely regarded as the cleanest full‑show capture.

key_highlights: "Lost Sailor > Saint Of Circumstance" early set pairing shows tight experimentation.
Second‑set peak: "He’s Gone" into Drum/Space → "Wharf Rat".

band_performance.Jerry: Supreme noodling on the "Jack Straw" opener and a perfect, soaring tone on "Sugaree", though a minority of listeners note his playing dips later in the set.
band_performance.Bob: One reviewer called Bob’s vocal delivery soulless on several numbers, but no other specific vocal critiques appear across recordings.
band_performance.Brent: Described as solidly implicated in the show’s vibe, providing steady keyboard texture throughout.
band_performance.Mickey: Drums segment highlighted as a strong point of the second set; Mickey locks into the groove alongside Bill.
band_performance.Billy: Both drummers deliver tight, propulsive rhythms during Drum/Space and maintain momentum into "Wharf Rat".
